title: Carved birch butter spreader
subtitle: A short seasoned-birch spreader shaped entirely with a sloyd knife
excerpt: A butter spreader is the fastest route from a piece of seasoned birch to a finished, useful kitchen item. Flat blade, short rounded handle, no bowl hollow: the whole piece is shaped with push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts in an afternoon. This tutorial covers profiling from a sawn blank, shaping the thin blade, and finishing with raw linseed oil.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the sloyd-knife shaping method on small flat utensils. Newey & Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1898) for grain selection in seasoned birch.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber brought to a stable moisture content. Indoor projects aim for 8 to 12 per cent; seasoned wood holds its shape once jointed or shaped.
The rough-cut piece of wood from which a finished item is carved or shaped.
A carving cut in which the blade travels away from the body, driven by the forearm. The default safe cut throughout the shaping stages.

projectSchedule.title: Shape the blank with the sloyd knife
projectSchedule.body: Profile, bevel, and refine the blade and handle. One session.

projectSchedule.title: Sand and apply first oil coat
projectSchedule.body: Sand to 240 grit, apply raw linseed oil, wipe off after one hour.

projectSchedule.title: Oil cured: food-safe
projectSchedule.body: Two weeks after oiling the spreader is food-safe.

content.text: A butter spreader is a small flat utensil: a thin flexible blade about 12 cm long, a short handle, and a gentle taper between them. This project uses
seasoned wood

rather than green, because the blade needs to hold a consistent thickness after drying. Birch is the right choice: it is food-safe, pale-finished, hard enough to stay firm across a thin blade, and widely available as small off-cuts or short boards. Sycamore is the closest alternative; cherry gives a slightly darker finish and a tighter grain. The entire piece is shaped with a
push cut
and thumb-pivot cut. No hook knife, no axe, no hollowing.
What you need: wood
A sawn
blank

of seasoned birch: 20 cm long, 4 cm wide, 12 mm thick. A short board, a straight-grained off-cut, or a purchased turning blank all work. The grain should run along the length of the blank, not across it; cross-grain in a 12 mm blade will break under flex. Check by looking along the end grain: the growth rings should curve gently side to side rather than arching across the width.
What you need: tools

A sloyd knife sharp enough to take a hair off the forearm. A leather strop to maintain the edge during the work. Fine sandpaper (180 grit then 240 grit) to smooth the blade face. Raw linseed oil to finish. Keep the strop on the bench or on the knee; strop every ten minutes of carving to keep the edge keen.
Safety

Four-finger grip on the work, thumb-push grip on the knife; every cut travels away from the body or across an arc that cannot reach a body part. A sharp knife is safer than a dull one: the dull blade skips and redirects unpredictably.
The work
Step 1: Draw the profile

Pencil the profile on the blank's wide face. The blade is 12 cm long, tapering from 35 mm at the shoulder down to 25 mm at the tip. The handle is 6 cm long and 30 mm wide, slightly convex on both long edges so it sits comfortably in the hand. The shoulder is a gentle concave curve, not a hard step.
Step 2: Rough out the side-view profile

Hold the blank in the four-finger grip and take push cuts along the long edges to bring the plan shape to the pencilled outline. The grain runs from tip to handle; push cuts from shoulder toward tip travel with the grain on the blade. Cuts from tip toward shoulder go against the grain and will lift chips: always cut in the correct direction, with the grain.
Step 3: Bevel the blade

The blade face is flat on one side and bevelled on the other, like a palette knife. Turn the blank edge-on and take push cuts down the length of the blade face, angling the knife at about fifteen degrees to the face. The bevel starts at the shoulder and runs to the tip. Work evenly from both long edges toward the centreline so the bevel is symmetric. Target a finished blade thickness of 3 to 4 mm at the tip, thinning from the full 12 mm at the shoulder over the first 3 cm of the blade.
Step 4: Shape and refine the handle

The handle is oval in cross-section. Take push cuts along the handle edges to round off the sharp sawn corners, then refine with thumb-pivot cuts to get a smooth oval. The shoulder between blade and handle should curve in a smooth concave arc; use the knife tip with short thumb-pivot cuts to refine it.
Step 5: Smooth the blade face

Sand the flat face of the blade along the grain: 180 grit to remove the knife marks, then 240 grit to bring the surface smooth to the touch. Do not sand the handle; the knife-finished surface is the right texture for grip. Brush off the sanding dust before oiling.
Finishing

Apply raw linseed oil generously to both faces, working it in with a cloth. Allow to penetrate for one hour, then wipe off all surface oil. Cure for two weeks before food contact. Raw linseed oil sets to a hard, food-safe film; it does not cure in a day. After curing, the birch will be a warm pale gold.
Variations
Make two or three from the same board blank so one is always available while others are setting. Linseed oil takes two weeks to set fully; stagger the projects so one is always ready.
Cherry gives a darker warm finish. The grain is tighter than birch and the blade holds a finer edge at the tip; reduce the tip thickness to 2 mm for a more flexible blade.
A left-handed spreader has the bevel on the opposite face. Swap the bevel direction at Step 3 and work accordingly.
Care
Hand wash, dry immediately. Never dishwasher, never soaking. Re-oil with a wipe of raw linseed every few months or when the surface looks dry.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems in shaping a thin blade.

items.symptom: The blade tip splits along the grain
items.cause: The grain was cross-grain, not running along the blank's length, or the tip was thinned too fast in too few passes.
items.fix: Start fresh with a blank where the grain visibly runs along the length. Thin the blade in many light passes rather than a few heavy ones.

items.symptom: The surface tears on the bevel
items.cause: The cut is going against the grain direction on the bevel. On birch, the grain can reverse direction near a knot.
items.fix: Flip the direction of the cut: work from shoulder toward tip, or from the long edge toward the centreline rather than the reverse. If both directions tear, the knife edge is dull; strop and retry.

items.symptom: The handle is not oval but has flats
items.cause: Push cuts have taken material unevenly, leaving flat planes rather than a smooth curve.
items.fix: Use short thumb-pivot cuts rather than full-length push cuts for the final shaping of the handle. Thumb-pivot cuts are easier to control on curves.
